narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which is close to the standard 3.21, you've put together a competitive profile in many ways, including coming from a middle-income family background. Your plan to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, also aligns with the norm. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 56.7%, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your record that stood out most strongly to the model. The biggest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower end compared to peers. Countering that somewhat was your strong LSAT score, which had the second-strongest pull in the model's thinking, nudging the prediction toward success because it shows sharp analytical skills above the typical student. Your undergraduate GPA provided a milder positive influence, as its near-average level helped a bit but wasn't enough to fully offset the concerns. Overall, these top three elements—your Year 3 deciles dominating the concern, tempered by your testing strengths—drove the decision more than other details like family income or enrollment status. For students like you, where later grades lag behind early potential and testing prowess, the model sees heightened bar exam risk compared to those with consistently higher school rankings. While this is disappointing, especially given barriers Black students often face in competitive academic tracks, it's not the end of the road. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your upper-year performance through targeted study habits or tutoring, perhaps retaking the LSAT if feasible to widen that advantage, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Many applicants in similar spots reapply successfully after addressing these gaps, so use this feedback as a roadmap to build an even stronger case.
